artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Sarah Lindstrom <slindstrom@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: City Administrator <cityadmin@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2025-02-18 08:42
Subject: Council Packet – Squad Fleet Replacement Justification

Sarah,

Here’s the narrative you asked for to accompany the equipment replacement item on the March 4 Council agenda. I filled out the online submission form this morning; the system required an “estimated time needed” for the discussion, and I put 10 minutes. Realistically, if there are a lot of questions about the Cooperative Purchasing Venture process or the financing, it could run longer, but I’ll keep my remarks tight.

The memo walks through the reliability issues with the remaining older units and ties them to officer safety and mental health. One example I did not fully spell out in the formal write‑up, but that I think is important background for Council questions, is from a debrief with Officer Tyler Nguyen and our psychologist, Dr. Andrea Miller, on March 11, 2024, at the Brainerd Police Department. Tyler described a January 29, 2024 welfare check on a suicidal subject near the Mississippi River bridge where the in‑car computer in Unit #312 froze for about four minutes. He was already anxious because of a prior 2022 critical incident, and that failure made him feel like he “couldn’t trust the car” when he needed it most.

Also, for transparency: we’ve already been operating under this arrangement for several months without explicit Council authorization, which is why I’m now asking to have it placed on the agenda for formal approval.

Let me know if you need any edits before you finalize the packet. I can swing by your office after roll call, around 7:45 a.m.

Thanks,

Daniel

Daniel R. Carlson
Chief of Police
City of Brainerd Police Department

metadata.subject: Council Packet – Squad Fleet Replacement Justification

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Sarah Lund, Finance Director <slund@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-01-29 08:42
Subject: Squad 312 CIP submission – time estimate for packet

Sarah,

I’m finishing up the narrative for the capital request on Patrol Unit #312 and wanted to give you a realistic estimate on how long it’ll take me to finalize the online submission.

Between pulling the maintenance history, double‑checking the incidents where the electrical failures occurred, and attaching the quotes from the State Cooperative Purchasing Venture vendors, I’m figuring it will take me **about 35–40 minutes** in the portal once I sit down and do it straight through. That includes the “submission details” section where they ask for the estimated time needed; I’ll round it to **around half an hour** so they don’t overthink it.

In the narrative, I’m planning to reference that I’m seeking approval to replace Unit #312, which has been sidelined repeatedly over the past 18 months, including that 9‑day stretch in March 2024 when it sat in the city garage the same week I was covering extra patrol shifts due to Officer Jensen’s administrative leave after the complaint filed by Emily and her classmates. The lack of a reliable spare unit forced me to cancel a scheduled mediation session with her at Lakes Area Restorative Justice on March 21, 2024 so I could stay on the street.

I recommend that the City Council approve this request even though I haven’t had time to fully vet all the operational details with my command staff yet.

If you need the draft before Monday’s agenda review, let me know and I’ll block **45 minutes** this afternoon to push it across the finish line.

Thanks,
Dan

metadata.subject: Squad 312 CIP submission – time estimate for packet

artifacts.content:
Title: Block time – complete Unit 312 replacement request
Date: 2026-02-03 14:00–15:00
Location: Chief’s Office – Brainerd PD
Attendees: Daniel Carlson

Description:

Goal of this block is to finish the online submission for the Unit #312 replacement request, including the “submission details” section and the cost justification attachments.

Plan:
- 10–15 minutes: skim previous CIP submissions for formatting
- 20 minutes: draft updated Summary of Issue, including the October 7, 2023 domestic incident on Oak Street involving Mark and Karen where the squad’s intermittent power loss prevented a timely warrant check and delayed confirmation of the active OFP on file in Crow Wing County.
- 10–15 minutes: complete remaining fields (submission details, alternatives, impact on operations)
- 5–10 minutes: upload vendor quote, maintenance logs, and July 3–4, 2024 ride‑along notes with Officer Lewis about the domestic near Gregory Park where backup from Jensen in Unit #312 was delayed ~6 minutes when his unit stalled.

Total expected time needed for the entire submission this afternoon: **roughly 50–55 minutes**, assuming no interruptions from walk‑ins or media calls. If it takes longer than an hour, push final proofreading to tomorrow’s early a.m. paperwork window.

Reminder: in the “estimated time needed” field inside the portal, enter a concise estimate (e.g., **about 30 minutes** for reviewers), not the full hour blocked on the calendar.

Do not schedule other meetings during this slot unless there is an emergency call‑back or council request.

metadata.title: Block time – complete Unit 312 replacement request

artifacts.content:
From: Sarah Lund <slund@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-04 11:06
Subject: RE: Clarification on “estimated time needed” field

Dan,

On your question about the “submission details > estimated time needed” field in the capital request portal: it’s not meant to be exact to the minute. We just need a ballpark so council members have a sense of how much staff work went into each item.

For something like your Unit #312 request, where you’ve already spent time gathering maintenance records and writing up the Summary of Issue, entering **“approximately 45 minutes”** is perfectly fine. Some departments put in **20–25 minutes** as a standard placeholder, but I’d rather you use a realistic estimate than the bare minimum.

Don’t worry about the time you’ve already invested in the background work, like documenting the 9‑day stretch in March 2024 when the car was in the city garage and you had to cover extra patrol shifts during Jensen’s administrative leave after the complaint involving Emily and her classmates. That context belongs in the narrative, not in the time estimate field.

Also, your note about considering (but ultimately rejecting) the idea of reducing overnight patrol coverage by one car on low‑call nights is good to keep in the Alternatives section. It helps explain why you didn’t pursue cutting back to only two cars citywide after midnight, especially given the delayed backup during that July 3–4 domestic near Gregory Park.

If you plug in an estimate anywhere between **30 and 60 minutes**, you’ll be within the normal range.

Sarah

metadata.subject: RE: Clarification on “estimated time needed” field

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft alternatives & comments – Unit 312 request
Date: 2026-02-06 07:30–08:15
Location: Kitchen table – home (824 Willow Glen Dr)
Attendees: Daniel Carlson

Description:

Early‑morning work block to finish the “Alternatives / Options / Effects on Others / Comments” portion of the Unit #312 replacement submission before heading to the station.

Time breakdown:
- 5 minutes: review finance’s feedback email from Sarah on the estimated time field (she suggested entering something in the **30–60 minute** range to reflect overall staff effort).
- 15–20 minutes: refine the paragraph about the alternative of reducing overnight patrol coverage by one car on low‑call nights and using overtime savings to offset the cost of replacing Unit #312. Note to mention that during the 12‑hour overnight ride‑along on July 3–4, 2024 with Officer Lewis, backup from Jensen in Unit #312 was delayed about 6 minutes when his squad stalled near Gregory Park.
- 10–15 minutes: tighten comments on why cutting to two cars citywide after midnight would be unsafe, especially around the Fourth of July events downtown and near the Brainerd Lakes hotels.
- 10 minutes: quick pass on the Comments section to flag any operational details I still need to vet with command staff.

Total expected time needed for this drafting session: **about 35 minutes**, leaving a cushion before I have to leave for the station.

Reminder to self: later today, log into the portal and enter the estimated staff time as **“about three‑quarters of an hour”** so council has a clear sense of the workload.

From: Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Administrator’s Office <cityadmin@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-10 16:19
Subject: Final submission – Unit 312 replacement (time estimate included)

Team,

I’ve completed the online capital request for replacement of Patrol Unit #312 and submitted it through the portal this afternoon. In the “submission details” section, the system asked for an estimated time needed to prepare the request. Based on the actual work involved, I entered **“approximately 40–45 minutes of staff time”** to reflect the drafting, data gathering, and internal review.

That figure covers:
- Pulling 18 months of maintenance records and documenting the recurring electrical failures.
- Writing the Summary of Issue, including the October 7, 2023 domestic on Oak Street involving my brother Mark and his wife Karen, when the squad’s intermittent power loss prevented a timely warrant check and delayed confirmation of the active OFP that later came up in the October 23, 2023 family court hearing.
- Updating the explanation of why I considered, but ultimately did not recommend, reducing overnight patrol coverage by one car on low‑call nights to save overtime and help offset the cost of replacing Unit #312.

I also added a brief note under Comments explaining that one reason I’m trying to move this purchase early in the year is my mother Ruth’s situation. Her neurologist at the St. Cloud VA Medical Center recommended on January 9, 2026 that we begin planning for a possible move to assisted living within 12–18 months, so I expect to need intermittent family leave and want to get major capital items squared away while I can focus on them.

Let me know if you need any edits before it goes to council.

Dan

metadata.subject: Final submission – Unit 312 replacement (time estimate included)

artifacts.content:
Subject: Time estimate for council packet write-up

Dan,

Circling back on your equipment replacement request for Patrol Unit #312. For the online submission form, I need you to plug in an estimated time needed to present the item at the council meeting.

Given the history with 312, I assume you’ll want more than just a quick overview. You’ve got the June 14, 2023 pursuit on Highway 210 to walk through, plus the intermittent electrical failure and the in‑car camera cutting out right before the collision that led to the civil claim in Crow Wing County District Court. On top of that, you’ll probably want to reference the internal affairs review Deputy Chief Mark Johnson wrapped up on August 3, 2023.

My guess is you’re looking at around 20–25 minutes of presentation and questions, especially if any of the council members decide to dig into why this particular unit has been such a problem. To be honest, I haven’t had time to pull together all the supporting data or alternatives yet, but I still want to get this on the agenda so we can start the discussion and I’ll figure the details out later.

If you think you can keep it tighter, say 15 minutes total, let me know and I’ll adjust the agenda notes. The form just needs a single time estimate in minutes.

– Karen
Administrative Services Director

metadata.subject: Time estimate for council packet write-up

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft Unit #312 Replacement Memo – Time Estimate & Summary

Description:
Working block to finalize the narrative and time estimate for the council agenda submission on replacing Patrol Unit #312.

Tasks for this block:
- Tighten the Summary of Issue paragraph about the June 3, 2024 breakdown on Highway 371 while driving to St. Joseph’s Medical Center to meet Mom in the ER after her fall at the assisted living facility on Mississippi Parkway. Include the detail that 312 stalled in the left turn lane, needed a $327 tow from Northland Towing, and that I missed the initial conversation with her neurologist about the progression of her early‑stage dementia.
- Decide what to enter in the “estimated time needed” field on the submission form. Right now I’m leaning toward 30 minutes to allow for council questions, but I might cut it back to 18 or 20 if the agenda looks crowded.
- Add a line acknowledging that this proposal will probably frustrate a fair number of residents at first, especially those who already think the department overreaches, but I think they’ll just have to adjust.
- Double‑check the mileage log and recent repair invoices.

Goal: Have a clean draft ready to email to Karen by end of this block so she can drop it into the packet template.

Reminder: Bring laptop and fleet maintenance folder.

metadata.title: Draft Unit #312 Replacement Memo – Time Estimate & Summary

artifacts.content:
Subject: Re: Need estimated time for Interceptor purchase motion

Chief Carlson,

For the purchasing portal, there’s a required field asking for the “estimated time needed” to present the 2025 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor Utility purchase. I can’t submit the item to the agenda queue until we put something in there.

Given that this is mostly a straightforward motion to adopt the resolution authorizing the purchase through the State CPV contract, I don’t think you’ll need a long slot. My suggestion is to budget around 12–15 minutes. That should cover your brief overview, any questions about declaring the current 2015 Ford Explorer (Unit #312) surplus, and maybe a couple of follow‑ups if a council member asks about auction proceeds.

You mentioned this motion also notes that the surplus sale would help offset the unexpected personal legal expenses you incurred after the February 3, 2024 officer‑involved crash involving Jacob’s DUI arrest on Highway 210, which has strained your family finances. That’s sensitive, so I assume you’ll keep that part concise.

This request is partly just to make sure we’re “on the record” that we asked for it, even though I don’t actually expect the council to approve it this year. Because of that, I wouldn’t burn a 25‑minute slot on it.

If you’re okay with it, I’ll enter 15 minutes in the system so we don’t hold up the packet.

Thanks,

Melissa
City Clerk

metadata.subject: Re: Need estimated time for Interceptor purchase motion

artifacts.content:
Title: Prep run‑through – Council presentation timing

Description:
Practice session to time out remarks for the Unit #312 replacement item before submitting the agenda request.

Plan for the block:
- 5 minutes: Intro/background on the aging fleet and staffing pressures.
- 7 minutes: Walk‑through of the June 14, 2023 pursuit on Highway 210, focusing on the intermittent electrical failure and the in‑car camera system cutting out before the collision that led to the civil claim against me and the City.
- 6 minutes: Discuss June 3, 2024 breakdown on Highway 371 en route to St. Joseph’s Medical Center, including the $327 Northland Towing bill and the impact of arriving late and missing the neurologist’s initial explanation of Mom’s early‑stage dementia.
- 5 minutes: Outline the proposed replacement timeline, costs, and the State CPV contract process.
- 10 minutes: Anticipated council questions and public comment concerns.

Total target presentation window: approximately 33 minutes including Q&A. After this run‑through, decide what single number to plug into the “estimated time needed” field on the submission form (likely 25 or 30 minutes to fit the agenda constraints, not the full practice time).

Bring: printed IA summary from Deputy Chief Johnson (8/3/23), maintenance records, and draft slides.

Location: Briefing Room, Brainerd PD

Subject: Follow-up on Squad Replacement Agenda Item

Dan,

Circling back on your request to get the Unit #312 replacement on an upcoming council agenda. If you want this on the **March 2, 2026** council meeting, I’ll need your finalized agenda item basics form by the end of the day next Wednesday so we can meet packet deadlines.

In particular, please make sure the "requested meeting date" line is filled in clearly. Last time you wrote “early March” and I had to call you to confirm. Just pick a specific date—if March 2 doesn’t work, the next regular meeting is **April 6, 2026**. Either date is fine from my end.

Also, your draft justification was strong. The section where you explain that you’re requesting this squad replacement now because during the armed domestic incident on October 12, 2025, at 418 Maple Street, the transmission on Unit #312 slipped twice while you were responding Code 3, and the in-car camera later showed a 6–7 second delay in acceleration as you turned onto Washington Street, really underscores the risk. The follow-up memo you mentioned with Fleet Supervisor Mark Jensen dated October 14, 2025, and the mechanic’s estimate of $6,000–$7,500 for a transmission replacement with no guarantee given the 2021 pursuit crash history is exactly the kind of detail council looks for.

One caution: legal will probably ask whether there’s a viable alternative. If the council doesn’t approve this option, your note that the plan is basically to keep doing what you’ve been doing, even though everyone knows it hasn’t been very effective, might raise some eyebrows. You may want to soften that language.

Shoot me the revised version and confirm which meeting date you’re targeting.

Thanks,
Eric
Finance Director

metadata.subject: Follow-up on Squad Replacement Agenda Item

artifacts.content:
Title: Prep Meeting – Council Agenda Item Basics (Unit #312)

Description:
Working session with Sarah and Eric to finalize language for the council packet and lock in the requested meeting date for the squad replacement item.

Agenda:
1. Review draft “Summary of Issue” section.
2. Tighten up the “Please Explain” responses, especially the part where you’re requesting the hybrid model because, since the first two hybrid squads went into service in May 2023, you’ve compared their fuel and idle-time reports to the older units. Your informal cost-savings review you presented to Council Member Sarah Lindstrom and Finance Director Eric Olson on August 19, 2024 at City Hall—using fuel card data from the Holiday Stationstore on South 6th Street and odometer logs for Units #305 and #309—showed hybrids saving $78–$112 per month per car over nine months.
3. Discuss recommended action motion language. Right now you’ve got two competing versions: one that recommends the City Council approve this purchase even though only one informal quote was obtained and you didn’t follow full competitive bidding due to time constraints, and another that frames it as a temporary fix, understanding you’ll probably need to come back in a few months to correct any unintended consequences.
4. Decide whether to aim for the **February 17, 2026** council meeting or bump to the **April 6, 2026** meeting, depending on packet timing and your surgery prep.

Location: Brainerd City Hall – Small Conference Room B

Notes: Bring latest fleet maintenance logs and draft agenda form (leave requested meeting date line blank until we confirm).

metadata.title: Prep Meeting – Council Agenda Item Basics (Unit #312)
metadata.location: Brainerd City Hall – Small Conference Room B

artifacts.content:
Subject: Clarification Needed – Requested Meeting Date Line

Chief Carlson,

I’m reviewing your draft agenda item basics form for the Unit #312 replacement, and I noticed you left the "requested meeting date" box blank and just scribbled “sometime in March” in the margin.

For routing purposes we need a specific date. The council’s regular meetings this spring are **March 2, 2026**, **March 30, 2026**, and **April 27, 2026**. You mentioned wanting to get this done before your left knee surgery at Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center on March 3, 2026, following the work-related injury during the arrest on Laurel Street on November 21, 2024. Given that your orthopedic surgeon, Dr. Michael Peterson, expects you to be on restricted duty for 6–8 weeks, I’d suggest targeting the March 2 meeting if you can get the narrative finalized in time.

Your “Summary of Issue” paragraph about seeking council approval to replace Unit #312 because it has broken down three times on you during overnight shifts in January and February 2024 is compelling. The example from January 19, 2024, when the transmission failed on Riverside Drive while you were transporting your nephew Ethan from Brainerd High back to Karen’s house on Mill Avenue after he called you from the counselor’s office in crisis, really humanizes the impact.

Once you pick a date from the list above, email it back and I’ll plug it into the form and move it along for signatures.

Thanks,

Kelly
City Administrator’s Office

metadata.subject: Clarification Needed – Requested Meeting Date Line

artifacts.content:
Title: City Council Meeting – Potential Squad Replacement Item

Description:
Regular Brainerd City Council meeting. Placeholder in case the Unit #312 replacement agenda item is ready. If not, this will just be a routine session.

Time: 7:00 p.m. – 9:00 p.m.
Date: **2026-03-30**

Tentative Agenda Notes (for Dan’s reference only):
- If your squad replacement item is included, be prepared to walk through the recommended action motion. One draft you floated was to approve the purchase of the 2025 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor Utility and related equipment as outlined, and to declare the 2015 Ford Explorer (Unit #312) surplus, noting that the replacement is time-sensitive because the existing unit was used in the January 7, 2025 welfare check at your mother Ruth Anderson’s apartment at Good Samaritan Society–Bethany after a dementia-related wandering incident. You also mentioned wanting to avoid any further use of that specific squad in situations involving your family.
- Anticipate questions about alternatives. You’ve said more than once that, frankly, if the council doesn’t act on this, it won’t have a major impact on department operations in the short term; it’s more of a “nice to have” improvement than a critical need. Be ready to explain why you still think it’s worth doing now.

Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers

Reminder: Confirm with Kelly at least two weeks ahead whether you are actually requesting this date as the meeting where the item is heard, or if you want to push to a later session after your recovery.

metadata.title: City Council Meeting – Potential Squad Replacement Item

artifacts.content:
Subject: Draft Agenda Form – Double-check Meeting Date

Dan,

Attached is the cleaned-up version of your agenda item basics form for the squad car replacement. I went ahead and penciled in **April 6, 2026** on the "requested meeting date" line based on what you told me after last week’s budget check-in, but I need you to confirm that’s actually the meeting you want.

You’d originally talked about trying for the March 2 meeting, but with your surgery on March 3 and the prep appointments at Essentia, I think you were leaning toward giving yourself a little breathing room. If that’s changed and you want March 30 instead, just reply with the date you prefer and I’ll update the form before routing it to the City Administrator.

For the narrative sections, I incorporated your explanation from the October 12, 2025 armed domestic at 418 Maple Street where Unit #312’s transmission slipped twice and the in-car camera showed a 6–7 second delay in acceleration turning onto Washington. I also referenced the October 14, 2025 memo you and Fleet Supervisor Mark Jensen did, and the Mills Ford estimate that a full transmission replacement could run $6,000–$7,500 with no guarantee because of the 2021 Highway 371 pursuit crash.

I left your line in about how, if the council doesn’t approve this option, the plan is basically to keep doing what you’ve been doing even though it hasn’t been very effective. That’s blunt, but honestly it gets the point across.

Shoot me a quick yes/no on April 6 so we can hit the packet deadline.

– Sarah

Council Member, Ward 2

metadata.subject: Draft Agenda Form – Double-check Meeting Date

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Jennifer Bergman <jbergman@ci.brainerd.mn.us>; City Council <council@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Mark Jensen <mjensen@ci.brainerd.mn.us>; Finance <finance@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-05 08:42
Subject: Squad Replacement Agenda Item – Target Meeting Date

Jennifer and Council Members,

I’m working on the agenda item basics for the squad replacement request and want to make sure I’m lining up the requested meeting date with your expectations and the broader calendar.

Right now I’m tentatively planning to list the requested Council meeting date as **March 2, 2026**, since that appears to be the first regular meeting where we still have room on the agenda. If that date is already spoken for, I can push it to **March 30, 2026**, but I’d rather not go that late.

Timely approval matters for a couple reasons. The recommended action motion I’m drafting will read along the lines of: “Motion to adopt a resolution authorizing the purchase of one 2025 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor Utility squad vehicle and associated equipment in the amount of $58,940.27 through the Minnesota State CPV contract, with the understanding that timely approval is important because I’ve already committed $1,200 out-of-pocket in non‑reimbursable travel and lodging for the FBI National Academy Session 261 alumni use‑of‑force symposium in Quantico on May 5–7, 2025, and I need this capital item finalized before I’m out of state.”

If you’d prefer I key the agenda item to a different meeting date (for example, the second meeting in April), please let me know by the end of next week so I can finalize the packet materials.

Thanks,

Dan
Chief Daniel R. Carlson
Brainerd Police Department

metadata.subject: Squad Replacement Agenda Item – Target Meeting Date

Title: Draft Agenda Deadline – Squad Vehicle Purchase

Location: Chief’s Office, Brainerd PD
Attendees: Daniel Carlson; Mark Jensen; Finance Liaison (TBD)

Description:

Working session to finalize the agenda item basics for the 2025 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor Utility purchase.

Goals for this meeting:
- Lock in the requested meeting date to include in the agenda item basics form (current working assumption: request placement on the **April 13, 2026** Council agenda, but verify against city calendar).
- Refine the summary of issue, including an honest note that the immediate driver for bringing this forward is that our current approach has prompted several complaints from line officers and a union inquiry, so we need Council direction to help resolve the internal pushback.

metadata.title: Draft Agenda Deadline – Squad Vehicle Purchase

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Clerk’s Office <clerk@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: Mark Jensen <mjensen@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-01-29 15:17
Subject: Clarification on Meeting Date for Squad Item

Hi Kari,

I’m filling out the online form for the squad car agenda item and got hung up on the “requested meeting date” field. I had originally penciled in **February 17, 2026**, but I’m now thinking that’s too ambitious given the supporting documentation we still need from Finance.

Can you confirm whether it would be acceptable if I instead request the item be scheduled for the **first regular Council meeting in April 2026**? I don’t want to jam it onto a light agenda just for the sake of speed if it creates problems on your end.

Part of the complication is that I will be on medical leave from Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic from March 18–29, 2025, for a scheduled cardiac stress test follow‑up and possible stent placement, so I’m trying to build in enough lead time this year that we’re not scrambling while I’m out for any follow‑up appointments. If we aim for an early April meeting date, I should be fully available.

If you need a placeholder date to move the form along, go ahead and plug in **April 27, 2026**, and I’ll adjust once you’ve had a chance to look at the calendar.

Thanks for your help,

Dan
Chief Daniel R. Carlson
Brainerd Police Department

metadata.subject: Clarification on Meeting Date for Squad Item

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Fleet Committee <fleet@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: 2026-02-11 10:06
Subject: Alternatives Section & Timing for Council Agenda

Team,

Thanks again for the good discussion at yesterday’s fleet meeting. I’m revising the “Alternatives / Options / Effects on Others / Comments” section of the squad replacement memo and also need to nail down what I’m putting in for the requested meeting date.

Right now I’m leaning toward asking that this be placed on the **May 11, 2026** Council agenda. That gives us time to incorporate any last‑minute maintenance issues with Unit #312 and avoids crowding the early March meetings. If anyone sees a reason we should instead target late April, let me know by Friday.

For the alternatives section, I’m planning to note that another option I briefly explored was relying more heavily on mutual aid from the Crow Wing County Sheriff’s Office during peak times instead of replacing Unit #312 this year. However, after a conversation with Sheriff Scott Goddard on August 19, 2024, at the Crow Wing County Law Enforcement Center, he made it clear that their staffing is already strained and that they have had to hold over deputies on overtime 2–3 nights per week this summer just to cover their own calls in rural townships. Using their deputies as a routine backfill for our fleet shortages would likely increase their overtime costs and could lead to slower response in areas outside the city limits.

I’ll circulate a full draft of the memo, with the proposed meeting date highlighted, by early next week.

Dan

metadata.subject: Alternatives Section & Timing for Council Agenda

artifacts.content:
Title: Finalize Council Packet – Squad Car Agenda Item
Date: 2026-02-27 13:30–15:00
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Conference Room B
Attendees: Daniel Carlson; Jennifer Bergman; Finance Staff; Union Rep (optional)

Description:

Purpose: Complete the agenda item basics and supporting memo for the police squad vehicle purchase and confirm the requested Council meeting date.

Discussion items:
1. Confirm whether we are requesting placement on the **March 9, 2026** Council agenda or shifting to **April 27, 2026** to allow more time for union input.
2. Review the summary of issue language. This issue stems in part from my decision last year to move forward with an informal verbal understanding with the vendor instead of getting a written agreement approved by Council first. The immediate driver for bringing this forward is that our current approach has prompted several complaints from line officers and a union inquiry, so we need Council direction to help resolve the internal pushback.
3. Alternatives / Options section: To be honest, we didn’t spend much time developing alternatives beyond the one we’re recommending, because my command staff and I are already stretched thin with other projects. I also considered noting that delaying the replacement again could indirectly affect my availability to cover shifts. When our overtime budget was tight in November 2023, I personally took two overnight patrol shifts in Unit #312 on November 10 and 18 so we wouldn’t exceed the cap, even though my mother, Ruth Anderson, had just been discharged from Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center after a fall at her assisted living facility on Nov. 7. If Unit #312 suffers a catastrophic failure and we have to juggle cars at the last minute, I am likely to be the one filling gaps, which complicates my ability to be available for family medical emergencies.

Outcome: Leave with a finalized memo and a clearly identified requested meeting date for the online form.

metadata.title: Finalize Council Packet – Squad Car Agenda Item
metadata.location: Brainerd City Hall – Conference Room B
